On the other hand, healthy cash flows can entice a company's management to indulge in things such as pet projects, lavish expenses, etc. But by levering the company up, the severity of this predicament is tempered. Companies with larger loads of debt are subject to higher interest payments and financing obligations. This reality forces management to be disciplined with their resources.

While some may knock Progress for its debt levels, particularly relative to other software companies, I don't think it's an apples-to-apples comparison. In a way, Progress is playing an entirely different game than these other companies. They're in the acquisitions business, while others are still chasing organic growth. As such, I don't think it's fair to knock Progress for their discrete capital structure.

Valuation

Seeking Alpha's quant system rates Progress' overall valuation as a 'C-.' Generally speaking, it's rated favorably in the metrics that start with EBITDA and move lower down the income statement, such as EBIT, earnings, and cash flow.

Currently, Progress trades for an EV/Revenue multiple of 5.1x, an EV/EBITDA multiple of 15x, and a Price/Cash Flow multiple of 12.9x. It's worth noting that Progress' year-over-year growth rates for revenue, EBITDA, and cash from operations are 12.9%, 16.6%, and 6.2%.

One company worth comparing with Progress is OpenText Corporation (OTEX). Much like Progress, OpenText is a serial acquirer of B2B software assets with recurring, stable revenues.

From the outside, OpenText can make Progress look relatively expensive. Currently, OpenText is trading for an EV/Revenue multiple of 2.4x, an EV/EBITDA multiple of 8.6x, and a Price/Cash Flow multiple of 8.5x. Each of these multiples are several turns lower than what Progress trades for. However, a closer look under the hood shows that Progress' economic engine is operating at a far superior clip than OpenText's.

Year-over-year revenue growth for OpenText is -11.5%. For a company that relies on debt financing to fund their acquisitions, much like Progress, shrinking revenues are a major concern. For this reason, I believe that OpenText's suppressed multiples relative to Progress are merited.

Another company worth comparing with Progress is Constellation Software (OTCPK:CNSWF). For those that are unfamiliar, Constellation Software is essentially the holy grail of acquisitive software holding companies. They've grown their strategy to impressive heights, as they now eclipse over $10 billion of annual revenue.

In many ways, I believe that Constellation represents the valuation ceiling for Progress. And as things stand, Constellation trades for an EV/Revenue multiple of 7.6x, an EV/EBITDA multiple of 41.3x, and a Price/Cash Flow multiple of 33.3x. But, it's important to note that these multiples are likely suppressed because Constellation trades on a Canadian exchange. If they were to instead be listed on a US exchange such as the NYSE or NASDAQ, they would likely command even higher multiples.

With this in mind, I don't think it would be unrealistic for Progress to trade for a multiple similar to that of Constellation's Canadian-exchange-suppressed multiple. Regardless, I believe that Progress currently trades at a fair price for long-term investors.

What to Watch for in Q2 Earnings

With Progress' Q2 earnings just around the corner, there are a couple of key developments that investors should monitor. First, the progress and performance of the ShareFile integration will be noteworthy. Progress' executives shared in their Q1 earnings call that their tuck-in of ShareFile was "on track or ahead of plan," and that the full integration should be complete by the close of fiscal year 2025.

The success of the ShareFile acquisition will play a key role in shaping Progress' future trajectory. One metric that investors can track to gauge the advancement of the integration is the business unit's operating margin. Management is targeting a 40% margin for the unit, largely fueled by synergies from the consolidation with Progress.

A second development to follow in the Q2 earnings release is Progress' ARR trajectory. From Q4 2024 to Q1 2025, Progress saw a marginal dip in their ARR. Anthony Folger, Progress' CFO, provided color on the occurrence:

"Sometimes Q4 to Q1, we will see a slight, I'll call it, a seasonal dip in ARR. And then we generally see that bounce back in the second quarter. If you were to look back at some of our Q1 earnings calls, maybe '23 or '22, you'd probably see a similar trend line. So nothing out of the ordinary from our perspective."

While this is reassuring commentary from Folger, the numbers never lie. Investors should closely watch the ARR number to see if it accelerates as it has in previous quarters and years.

Risks

It's no secret that Progress is in the notoriously difficult M&A business. Progress' future rides on their continued ability to find, fund, and integrate acquisitions. Any of those steps along the way can introduce bumps or roadblocks that could potentially derail their growth strategy. And with the large recent acquisition of ShareFile, the integration risk looms large.

Progress' management issued sizable sums of debt to fund the acquisition. And of course, they believe it will be a net-positive outcome, or else they wouldn't have wasted their resources on it. Regardless, their ability to extract the synergies that they planned for will be key for the integration. Should they fall short on their targets, Progress stock could take a hit. But if the past is a worthwhile indicator, Progress is well-equipped.

Conclusion

Progress has built a compelling business. They have become serial acquirers of software business, and operate their company with the financial acumen that you'd expect from an M&A-reliant firm. They're more than willing to take big swings, such as the ShareFile acquisition, and have demonstrated their ability to turn those bets into accretive outcomes. Moreover, their valuation appears reasonable, if not slightly undervalued, relative to peers. In all, they paint a compelling case for a 'Buy' rating for long-term investors.
